content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 4, 1991




Gaddi H. Vasquez, Chairman
County of Orange Board of Supervisors
10 Civic Center Plaza
Santa Ana, CA 92701-4061

                                          Re: Your Request for Advice
                                              Our File No. A-91-505

Dear Mr. Vasquez:

     We have received your letter dated November 4, 1991, in which
you seek advice regarding the "mass mailing" provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ We provide you with advice
pursuant to Regulation 18329, based upon the facts provided in
your letter.

                              QUESTION

     May you, as an elected member of the board of supervisors,
send a mailer of more than 200 at public expense informing both
new and former constituents of the recent changes in supervisorial
boundaries?

                              CONCLUSION

     Because such a mailing would be considered a mass mailing for
purposes of the Act, you may not use public funds to send the
mailing unless an exception to the prohibition applies.

                              FACTS

     You are an elected member of the County of Orange Board of
Supervisors. As a result of redistricting, you wish to notify
constituents whom you no longer represent, as well as those whom
you now represent, of the new district boundaries.



___________
1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-91-505
Page 2


ANALYSIS

Section 89001 prohibits a newsletter or other mass mailing
from being sent at public expense. A "mass mailing" is over two
hundred substantially similar pieces of mail sent in a calendar
month. (Section 82041.5.)

Regulation 18901 implements Section 89001 and clarifies that
the purpose of the mass mailing restriction is to prevent elected
officers from using public funds to further their candidacy for
reelection by sending out newsletters and other mass mailings.
(Lagorio Advice Letter, I-91-185; Krannawitter Advice Letter, A-
89-100, Symkowick Advice Letter, I-88-399.) The fact that the
mailing might otherwise be factually correct or fulfill a public
purpose is not determinative for purposes of the prohibition.

Affiliation

As an elected member of the County of Orange Board of
Supervisors, you are a public official for purposes of the Act.
(Section 82048.) The mailing which you wish to send would be paid
for with public of the County, the governmental entity with which
you are affiliated. Regulation 18901 provides that a mass mailing
may not be sent within the meaning of Section 89001 if, among
other things, the item sent either features an elected officer
affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing
(Regulation 18901(a)(2)(A)), or includes the name or other
reference to an elected officer affiliated with the agency which
produces or sends the mailing and is prepared in consultation
with the elected officer. (Regulation 18901(a)(2)(B).)

The contemplated mailing would fall within the mass mailing
restrictions, because you are the elected official affiliated with
the agency that sends the mailing, your name is included in the
mailing, and the mailing is being prepared in consultation with
you. Therefore, assuming the other requirements of Regulation
18901(a) have been met, it appears that the mailing could not be
sent at public expense unless one of the exceptions is applicable.

If, however, you yourself wish to send such a communication
only to your present and/or former constituents, you may use
existing campaign funds for such purposes. (Section 89512.)

File No. A-91-505
Page 3

Exceptions

Regulation 18901(b) lists several exceptions to the
prohibition of Section 89001. None of the exceptions contained in
Regulation 18901(b) appear applicable to your query.²

This is not to say that the information which you wish to
convey to your present and/or former constituent cannot be
communicated by other methods. It only means that you may not use
public funds to communicate directly to present and/or former
constituents of your supervisorial district in the manner you have
suggested in your November 4, 1991 letter.

For example, Regulation 18901(b)(1) permits elected officers'
names to appear in a mailing in a letterhead or logotype of the
agency's stationery and forms. It also permits the names to
appear in a roster listing when they appear in the same
typeface, type size, type color, and location. A roster that
contains the names of the elected district directors in the same
typeface, size and location as the District's executive officers
is in compliance with the mass mailing provisions of the Act.
(Steffen Advice Letter, A-90-033; Biondo Advice Letter, A-89-022.)

Thus, a mailing sent to all registered voters of Orange
County on county letterhead that included a roster listing of all
supervisors would comply with the exception provided by Regulation
18901(b)(1).

_________________________

² The exceptions include roster-listing in agency letterhead
(Regulation 18901(b)(1), media press releases (Regulation
18901(b)(2)), communications with other governmental entities
(Regulation 18901(b)(3)), intra-agency communications (Regulation
18901(b)(4)), mailings pertaining to the payment or collection of
funds (Regulation 18901(b)(5)), governmental program information
(Regulation 18901(b)(6)), legal notices (Regulation 18901(b)(7)),
telephone directories or organizational charts (Regulation
18901(b)(8)), meeting or event announcements (Regulation
18901(b)(9)), agendas and other writings required to be made
available (Regulation 18901(b)(10)), and business cards
(Regulation 18901(b)(11)).

File No. A-91-505
Page 4


I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have further questions regarding this matter,
or wish to supplement your inquiry with additional facts, please
contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Jonathan S. Rothman
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JSR:ken

sections.analysis:
Section 89001 prohibits a newsletter or other mass mailing from being sent at public expense. A "mass mailing" is over two hundred substantially similar pieces of mail sent in a calendar month. (Section 82041.5.)

Regulation 18901 implements Section 89001 and clarifies that the purpose of the mass mailing restriction is to prevent elected officers from using public funds to further their candidacy for reelection by sending out newsletters and other mass mailings. (Lagorio Advice Letter, I-91-185; Krannawitter Advice Letter, A-89-300, Symkowick Advice Letter, I-88-399.) The fact that the mailing might otherwise be factually correct or fulfill a public purpose is not determinative for purposes of the prohibition.

Affiliation

As an elected member of the County of Orange Board of Supervisors, you are a public official for purposes of the Act. (Section 82048.) The mailing which you wish to send would be paid for with public of the county, the governmental entity with which you are affiliated. Regulation 18901 provides that a mass mailing may not be sent within the meaning of Section 89001 if, among other things, the item sent either features an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing (Regulation 18901(a)(2)(A)), or includes the name or other reference to an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing and is prepared in consultation with the elected officer. (Regulation 18901(a)(2)(B).)

The contemplated mailing would fall within the mass mailing restrictions, because you are the elected official affiliated with the agency that sends the mailing, your name is included in the mailing, and the mailing is being prepared in consultation with you. Therefore, assuming the other requirements of Regulation 18901(a) have been met, it appears that the mailing could not be sent at public expense unless one of the exceptions is applicable.

If, however, you yourself wish to send such a communication only to your present and/or former constituents, you may use existing campaign funds for such purposes. (Section 89512.)

Exceptions

Regulation 18901(b) lists several exceptions to the prohibition of Section 89001. None of the exceptions contained in Regulation 18901(b) appear applicable to your query.2

This is not to say that the information which you wish to convey to your present and/or former constituents cannot be communicated by other methods. It only means that you may not use public funds to communicate directly to present and/or former constituents of your supervisorial district in the manner you have suggested in your November 4, 1991 letter.

For example, Regulation 18901(b)(1) permits elected officers' names to appear in a mailing in a letterhead or logotype of the agency's stationery and forms. It also permits the names to appear in a roster listing when the names appear in the same typeface, type size, type color, and location. A roster that contains the names of the elected district directors in the same typeface, size and location as the District's executive officers is in compliance with the mass mailing provisions of the Act. (Steffen Advice Letter, A-90-033; Biondo Advice Letter, A-89-022.)

Thus, a mailing sent to all registered voters of Orange County on county letterhead that included a roster listing of all supervisors would comply with the exception provided by Regulation 18901(b)(1).

2 The exceptions include roster-listing in agency letterhead (Regulation 18901(b)(1)), media press releases (Regulation 18901(b)(2)), communications with other governmental entities (Regulation 18901(b)(3)), intra-agency communications (Regulation 18901(b)(4)), mailings pertaining to the payment or collection of funds (Regulation 18901(b)(5)), governmental program information (Regulation 18901(b)(6)), legal notices (Regulation 18901(b)(7)), telephone directories or organizational charts (Regulation 18901(b)(8)), meeting or event announcements (Regulation 18901(b)(9)), agendas and other writings required to be made available (Regulation 18901(b)(10)), and business cards (Regulation 18901(b)(11)).

I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you requested. If you have further questions regarding this matter, or wish to supplement your inquiry with additional facts, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.
